perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, in the event that the applicable Party decides not to file at all or not to file a continuing or other application to maintain the viability of the U.S part of a family of patents to which an application belongs, or decides to abandon or discontinue the prosecution or maintenance of any of the Joint IP Rights, such Party shall notify the other Party thereof, and such other Party may elect to continue the prosecution (including non-provisional application and PCT entry) or maintenance of such Joint IP Rights at its sole expense and in the name(s) of both Stryker and Conformis.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, in the event that the applicable Party decides not to file at all or not to file a continuing or other application to maintain the viability of the U.S part of a family of patents to which an application belongs, or decides to abandon or discontinue the prosecution or maintenance of any of the Joint IP Rights, such Party shall notify the other Party thereof within 30 days of taking this decision, and such other Party may elect to continue the prosecution (including non-provisional application and PCT entry) or maintenance of such Joint IP Rights at its sole expense and in the name(s) of both Stryker and Conformis.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a 30-day notification requirement could conflict with the timeframe required to take action on a patent application to avoid abandonment in the USPTO. If the decision to abandon is made close to a deadline, 30 days might not give the other party enough time to act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 37 CFR § 1.134 - Time for reply or appeal
perturbation.law_explanation: 37 CFR § 1.134 specifies that the time for reply to an Office action, or for taking an appeal, in a patent application is generally three months, but can be shortened to two months. The added 30-day notification requirement before another party can take over prosecution could leave insufficient time to respond to an Office action, potentially leading to abandonment of the patent despite the other party's willingness to maintain it, thus impacting the validity of the IP.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 37 CFR § 1.134 - Time period for reply to an Office action. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 37 CFR § 1.134 - Time period for reply to an Office action. CFR prev | next § 1.134 Time period for reply to an Office action. An Office action will notify the applicant of any non-statutory or shortened statutory time period set for reply to an Office action. Unless the applicant is notified in writing that a reply is required in less than six months, a maximum period of six months is allowed. [ 62 FR 53194 , Oct. 10, 1997] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day notification period before a party can act on patent rights. The Law Explanation, supported by 37 CFR § 1.134 (Snippet 1 & 2), specifies time periods for replying to Office actions can be as short as two months. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this 30-day notification might not leave enough time to act, potentially leading to abandonment. This contradicts the law because the added requirement could cause non-compliance with statutory deadlines for patent prosecution, making the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: If any Party believes the other is in material breach of any of its material obligations under this Agreement in a manner other than as set forth in Article III to which this Section 8.2(b) does not apply, it may give notice of such material breach to the allegedly breaching Party, which Party shall have [**] (or such longer period of time as may be reasonably commensurate with the effort reasonably required to remedy such default) in which to remedy such default. If such alleged material breach is not remedied in the time period set forth above, the Party alleging material breach shall refer the matter to the chief executive officers of each Party, who shall meet and confer within [**] after notice from the non-breaching Party of its desire for such a meeting. If the Parties are unable to resolve any dispute in such meeting and no Action has been brought in accordance with Section 10.8 with respect to such dispute, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon delivery to the defaulting Party of a Termination Notice.
perturbation.changed_text: If any Party believes the other is in material breach of any of its material obligations under this Agreement in a manner other than as set forth in Article III to which this Section 8.2(b) does not apply, it may give notice of such material breach to the allegedly breaching Party, which Party shall have five (5) days (or such longer period of time as may be reasonably commensurate with the effort reasonably required to remedy such default) in which to remedy such default. If such alleged material breach is not remedied in the time period set forth above, the Party alleging material breach shall refer the matter to the chief executive officers of each Party, who shall meet and confer within five (5) days after notice from the non-breaching Party of its desire for such a meeting. If the Parties are unable to resolve any dispute in such meeting and no Action has been brought in accordance with Section 10.8 with respect to such dispute, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon delivery to the defaulting Party of a Termination Notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to five days may be considered unconscionable or unreasonable, particularly if the breach involves complex issues or requires significant time to rectify. This alteration may impede fair resolution and due process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding reasonableness and good faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 1-304 states that "Every contract or duty within [the Uniform Commercial Code] imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Imposing an unreasonably short cure period may be seen as violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which is a component of contract law in New York and other jurisdictions. A court could find that five days is insufficient time to cure a material breach, rendering the clause unenforceable.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for a material breach to five days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be unconscionable or unreasonable, hindering fair resolution. The Law Explanation, referencing New York UCC § 1-304, asserts that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes UCC § 1-304, stating, "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." An unreasonably short cure period potentially violates this implied covenant, making the clause unenforceable. The modified contract, by imposing a potentially insufficient cure period, contradicts the obligation of good faith mandated by the UCC.
